chunk_summary: Guidelines for managing outpatient SAM in India, focusing on escalation criteria, defaulting risks, and discordant anthropometry decisions to ensure safe and effective care.
learning_objectives: Understand the criteria for escalating SAM cases from outpatient to inpatient care.
Identify strategies to reduce defaulting in outpatient CMAM programs in India.
Learn how to manage discordant anthropometry measurements for enrollment and discharge decisions.
key_facts: Escalate to inpatient care if danger signs like oedema, severe dehydration, or persistent anorexia are present.
High defaulting rates occur when travel is difficult or community referral is weak; ASHA workers can reduce this risk.
MUAC and WHZ identify different risk groups; both should be used for enrollment and discharge decisions.
Do not discharge based on a single marker: ensure both MUAC and WHZ have normalized.
Incorporate adherence questions at every visit to identify and address barriers to treatment.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: Large Indian program experience has shown high defaulting when travel is difficult or when community referral is weak; referrals by frontline workers such as ASHA reduce default risk.
document_key_topics: Outpatient escalation criteria for SAM management
Discordant anthropometry in enrollment and discharge decisions
